data.title: Launching Main Street Alpha: Focusing On Long-Term Wealth Creation
data.description:
Introduction

Today, I'm extremely excited to launch Main Street Alpha. This new investing service on Seeking Alpha was created to help investors focus on long-term wealth creation through macro-driven research, structural market trends, and high-conviction investment ideas.

Main Street Alpha combines deep macroeconomic analysis, geopolitical insights, and company-level research to identify the opportunities that benefit from long-term trends instead of short-term market noise. Members receive exclusive research reports, high-conviction investment ideas, three model portfolios, direct access to me, and a private member chat where members discuss the markets and long-term investment strategies.

For a limited time, the first group of members for Main Street Alpha can sign up for the service at the price of $499. The price increases to $599 after this special offer expires.

Main Street Alpha represents the next stage of the journey my followers and I have been on together for more than 10 years on Seeking Alpha.

Introducing Main Street Alpha

I started investing at the age of 15. That was 15 years ago. As some of you may know (because I have brought it up so much in my research), what I did back then was flat-out gambling. I still remember how I felt when I nearly blew up my account on Christmas Eve and had to pretend everything was fine during the holiday.

This is obviously an incredibly silly story, yet in hindsight, all of these mistakes were terrific building blocks for what turned out to be a career in finance and publication. Moreover, because I experimented with almost every type of investing and trading style/strategy, I was able to early on figure out the best approach that fit my goals and strategy.

Fast forward to 2026. I have spent the past six years working professionally in the industry, advising a hedge fund, working for a number of third-party research companies, and building a dividend growth portfolio that has become the foundation of a serious long-term investment strategy.

That strategy is built around what I like to call the “Big Picture” approach, as it combines macroeconomic research, structural trends, political developments, competitive business models, and durable wealth-building strategies like dividend growth investing.

My goal has always been to help investors filter out the daily market noise and focus on the factors that actually drive long-term returns. Even if some readers disagree with a specific stock pick or a thesis, I want them to walk away with a better understanding of the bigger picture that helps them in their own investment process.

Now, it's time to take all of this to the next level. I believe that everything I have done so far has led to this big moment, as I get to introduce my new endeavor called “Main Street Alpha,” which I'm launching here on Seeking Alpha with Albert Marko, my business partner, long-term mentor, and friend.

It's everything we have done over the past 10 years, packaged and brought to a whole new level, to bring institutional-grade research to the masses.

Why We Created Main Street Alpha

The current market environment seems to be louder and more challenging than ever.

Every day is a bombardment of news headlines, economic numbers, and a ton of narratives that have the potential to change everything happening in the markets. Essentially, the financial media cycle is moving faster, which is further worsened by social media-fueled noise and artificial intelligence. It has created a data overload.

In my personal experience, some of the most successful investors are the ones who filter out that noise, maintain a focus on the Big Picture (there it is again), and build portfolios that work for them. One thing a former mentor always told me was, “It’s all noise.”

That philosophy has always been the core of my work here on Seeking Alpha.

However, over time, it became increasingly clear to me that my readers wanted more than just ideas on single stocks. They wanted a place to discuss ideas in a more in-depth way, at a higher frequency, and with more in-depth analysis. And personally, I also wanted more. It was time for a change.

This reminds me of the following quote from the 1987 movie “Wall Street”:

If you're not inside, you are outside, OK?

We're going to change that, as we'll be that helping hand in a noisy market that helps you focus on the drivers that actually matter for long-term returns. To achieve that, we'll combine macroeconomic analysis, structural investment trends, and individual company research.

To us, the goal is very straightforward:

Help individual investors approach markets with the same big-picture mindset used by professional investors.

What You'll Find Inside Main Street Alpha

Main Street Alpha is built on a number of core pillars, all of which have the goal to help you make better long-term decisions.

First of all, our members will receive high-conviction investment ideas. All of these are fully supported by detailed research and Big-Picture analysis. Our research is designed to find the best companies and discuss major macro and geopolitical trends. Essentially, in our service, we go the extra mile and tell you what the “big guys” on Wall Street and in Washington, D.C., are discussing. Suddenly, you're an “insider” as well.

Second, we publish deep-dive research reports that go beyond the “traditional” articles you may be used to on the Internet. Our research deep-dives into various industries, macroeconomic developments, and niche investment opportunities that often go unnoticed.

Just think about the in-depth research I have written so far, taken to the next level.

Moreover, our members will get access to three portfolios:

  • Leo's personal portfolio.
  • A dividend income portfolio with a >5% average yield and high-single-digit annual dividend growth.
  • A community portfolio that is funded with service proceeds and directly influenced by members who get a say in what we buy and sell.

On top of that, members get full access to a private chat with direct access to me, the community, and any real-time investment alerts we publish.

In other words, we're going exclusive, to a whole new level, and you're invited!

Who's Behind Main Street Alpha

This service is run by Albert Marko and myself.

Most of you will be familiar with me. Not only did I just share my story with you, but I also spent the past 10 years sharing way too many details about my personal life. I'm sometimes surprised by how much people know about me and by how much people care.

Albert, however, is a name you may not be familiar with. Until now, of course.

I have worked with Albert for more than six years in various settings. He brings a different but highly complementary skill set, as he's a political-economic consultant and investor with roughly two decades of experience in foreign affairs and geopolitical analysis.

Moreover, throughout his career, he has advised hedge funds, corporations, and institutional investors on political risk, legislative developments, and international relations. His work has also involved consulting policymakers and members of Congress on geopolitical strategy and economic policy.

For a Big Picture approach, there's no better partner.

Hence, by integrating political analysis with macroeconomic research and equity strategy, Main Street Alpha aims to give investors a clearer view of the forces that truly drive long-term returns and generate long-term alpha and income for dividend investors.

Who Main Street Alpha Is For

This service is for everyone who has money invested or plans to invest.

However, to narrow it down, it's especially ideal for readers who:

  • Focus on long-term wealth creation.
  • Want to understand macro and geopolitical trends.
  • Appreciate dividend growth and income investing.
  • Prefer research-driven investment ideas.

If you're a short-term trader, this service may not be for you, as we help people achieve long-term goals and do not focus on short-term trading signals. However, even if you’re a trader and want to get a better understanding of the market, this service is for you as well.

After all, even traders need to understand the bigger picture.

A Quick Note on Expectations

Before wrapping things up, I want to make one more thing clear.

I'm a macro/equity strategist, not a salesman. This service is not about chasing the next hot stock or promising you quick gains. Markets do not work that way, and anyone who claims otherwise may be selling you something other than good research.

Some ideas will work extremely well. Others may take a while to unfold. And, occasionally, we'll be wrong on things. That's part of investing.

What matters is the process to increase the risk/reward and build something that works for you.

That's what we are about.

Join Us On The Next Stage Of The Journey

Over the past 10 years, the Seeking Alpha community has played a major role in shaping the way I think about markets and investing.

Main Street Alpha is the next step in the investment journey.

It's the place where we take everything we have been doing to a whole new level. Better intel, better research, portfolio management, and fruitful community discussions.

If that sounds like the investing approach you are looking for, we invite you to join us.

Main Street Alpha is available for $599 per year, with a limited launch discount for the first 400 members who will get the whole package for $499.

data.title: Bank Of America: Heading Into Q1 With More Priced In Than Left To Price
data.description:
The investment thesis for Bank of America (BAC) today seems to me to be leaning on the durability of net interest income growth in an environment that is beginning to change.

The bank continues to deliver solid results, with NII still healthy and resilient and a well-diversified business base. But the market has clearly stopped looking at what has been delivered recently and has started to question what is sustainable from now on. It is in this gap between perception and reality that I see the opportunity starting to form as the 1Q26 earnings approach (scheduled for April 15, before the opening bell).

At the same time, BAC no longer seems to me to be just a pure "spread bank," and this may not yet be well embedded in its share price. The evolution of fee businesses—wealth and capital markets, more specifically—suggests a more balanced model throughout the cycle and with the ability to cushion part of a normalization of the NII. This particular NII normalization, on the other hand, seems to me well embedded in BAC's share price.

I don't see BAC as an obvious or immediate bull case, but in a scenario where the worst already seems widely priced in, the setup starts to get more interesting for those who are willing to wait for this confirmation.

The Market Has Already Moved On

In the last earnings season, broken down by sub-segments, bank revenues came as follows, as depicted in the chart below.

It is interesting how there is a low correlation between the 4Q25 revenue performance and year-to-date stock returns of these large U.S. banks. In a way, it reinforces the idea that the market is already looking far beyond the recent results and focused on the durability of earnings in a seemingly shifting macro environment.

As ~59% of BAC's revenues come from the consumer and wealth management sides of the business, this is a bank with a greater exposure in consumer spending than other "Big Five" Banks of the U.S. economy. Thus, in a K-shaped economy, the reading for Bank of America becomes more nuanced, with arguably a slightly greater hint of uncertainty about earnings quality.

Reading the Macro Through BAC

In a bottom-up macro reading, the last Bank of America conference at RBC Capital Markets in March offered a macro reading, on the part of the bank, which I would classify as "resilient, but not clean".

The bank implicitly reiterates that the U.S. economy remains resilient in the aggregate, with healthy consumption and credit, but with clear signs of inequalities (K-shaped) and an environment that requires discipline and selectivity.

Starting with the still firm consumer. Spending has grown 5% YoY, with spending more focused on travel and entertainment, and with the consumer still with excess deposits (+13% vs. pre-COVID levels). This is not a slowing economy and not even close to a hard landing. The important nuance, however, is that in a K-shaped economy, high income spends more and low income is more pressured. On one end, this is connected with inflation, where part of the economy still has strong demand, and services inflation remains high (3.1% in February).

The credit market remains super healthy (for now) with average portfolio FICO at 780+, low exposure to bad credit, and no sign of relevant deterioration, also indicating the system is far from stress.

Business activity is still going on, according to early Q1 run-rate indicators. Lending "strong out the gate" with capital markets seeing IB growing 10% YoY and Markets in low double digits—no M&A freeze and no extreme risk aversion environment. Net interest income (NII) growing at 7% YoY and stable deposits show that interest rates are still high enough to benefit banks.

Implicitly, Bank of America's management team conveys an idea of how it reads the Fed that the economy does not need stimulus due to strong consumption, healthy credit, and growing lending. But it's also not overheating based on moderate spending growth and K-shaped Dynamics, which could eventually leave the Fed locked in for longer.

Building A More Balanced Earnings Model

Bank of America continues with the credit growth strategy, but in this case, selectively with high quality and underwriting refined by AI. No chasing growth. FY25 showed loans growing 8% YoY and deposits 3% YoY. CET1 came in at 11.4%, above the low.

The focus has clearly been on efficiency (146bps YoY improvement in efficiency ratio reaching 62%) and operating leverage, with guidance for FY26 of +200bps in operating leverage (currently at 2.5%), with frugality in costs reinforcing the view of a good environment, but not "easy money".

The strong bet seems to be on fee businesses. Wealth management has been growing, and capital markets have been active, hedging against any fall in NII in the future. Asset management fees grew 12% YoY, sales & trading up 11% YoY in FY25. IB fees and NII both growing by 7% YoY (and with a guidance of 5-7% in FY26) also show that Bank of America is already diversified.

Being historically a deposit-funded spread bank (and still trading at a multiple pegged to it), Bank of America has sought a model prepared for multiple interest rate scenarios. So much so that the strong NII model in high interest rates and strong fees for a low interest rate scenario/normalization seems to be playing out in practice, as ROE and ROTCE at 10.6% and 14.2% (solid profitability) were posted in FY25.

A Word Ahead of Q1

To beat the market estimates, Bank of America will need to post EPS numbers in excess of $1.00 (which would be 11.3% YoY growth) and revenues in excess of $29.77 billion (which would be 8.8% YoY growth).

The consensus looks slightly constructive over the past three months, especially in terms of earnings, having revised that figure upwards by ~1%. Precisely the NII guidance coming better than feared and rate cuts not yet hitting full in the P&L are arguably the main drivers of this slight optimism for 1Q26.

I would also say that the BAC reiterating that stronger Markets and IB at the beginning of Q1 also looks like something that surprises positively, since the market was afraid of a slowdown.

But the consensus being revised up by just 1% for 1Q26 shows only slightly less bearish. The drop in BAC's market cap by 10% from January to March 6 seems to me to be an indication of the market devaluing the peak of the NII (7.2% YoY growth in 2025), with the 2026 guidance, although strong, showing normalization at the end-point.

That being said, I believe that looking at 1Q26 and trying to find answers to BAC's rate sensitivity (in this case, still ~$2 billion impact per 100bps), whether additional hedges or stickier deposits could make this more constructive. In this case, I would pay attention to the fee businesses (where the hedge needs to prove) with wealth and Markets managing to sustain the growth of double digits.

Finally, BAC comes to report earnings trading as a kind of "run-of-the-mill" at 11.3x forward earnings (and a long-term PEG of 0.86, attractive) and also at 1.28x book value—all numbers similar to industry averages.

Risks and Final Remarks

I don't think the timing is an obvious "pound the table" moment for a strong bull case in BAC.

Shares are trading weakly, but also very clearly reflecting a wait-and-see setup. This is the case as we can see from the 2-year Treasury at 3.8%, the 10-year at 4.33%, and the 10y-2y curve at +0.51 p.p.

That is, the curve is already positively sloped, and possibly no longer inverted in this stretch. This suggests that the market is not pricing in further strong tightening from the Fed, but it is also not buying a large immediate monetary easing.

In any case, I think the BAC may post a relatively safe quarter, but the central point remains the NII's trajectory throughout 2026 and the bank's ability to sustain growth via fee businesses in a potentially lower interest rate environment.

The good news is that in terms of valuations, Bank of America already looks largely "de-risked" with trailing multiples well aligned with its historical averages after recently testing P/B levels close to 1.5x. On the one hand, the suggestion is that the compression of multiples may have already occurred and embeds the normalization of the NII, reflecting well the "good but not great" environment.

Lastly, the opposite of this scenario is the main risk of the BAC thesis. I would argue that the fact that its earnings remain more rate-dependent than the market is comfortable with, i.e., that the NII slows faster than expected and other engines fail to compensate, may cause a prolongation of the bearish streak for Bank of America's shares.

I don't believe this is the playbook at the moment, and I see the risk as more balanced from current levels. Therefore, I reiterate a moderately constructive view and my Buy recommendation for Bank of America.

data.title: Fed Walks A Policy Tightrope As Iran Conflict Clouds The Outlook
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is steering through one of the most challenging policy environments in years as the war with Iran destabilizes global energy markets and raises uncertainty about inflation and economic activity. The spike in geopolitical volatility leaves policymakers navigating an exceptionally narrow path: tightening too aggressively risks tipping the economy into recession, while easing too soon could reignite inflation. For the near term, the least worst option is to sit tight and leave rates unchanged until the incoming data start to make a strong case for changing the monetary policy bias.

The Cleveland Federal Reserve president, Beth Hammack, underscored this point in an interview with AP on Monday. Noting that keeping the Fed’s target rate steady for the near term “for quite some time” is her preference, “I can foresee scenarios where we would need to reduce rates... if the labor market deteriorates significantly,” she added. “Or I could see where we might need to raise rates if inflation stays persistently above our target.”

The Treasury market has recently recalibrated its outlook and is now firmly pricing in a higher probability of a rate hike in the near term after an extended run of dovish pricing. The policy-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y)(3.84% as of Apr. 6) continues to trade above the median effective Fed funds rate (3.64%), marking a clear change in sentiment toward a hawkish bias for the first time since 2022.

The degree of future inflation risk remains debatable, as does the potential for a slowdown in economic growth. But the odds are widely perceived as higher for one or both sides of this coin.

“All roads now lead to higher prices and slower growth,” predicts IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva. “We are in a world of elevated uncertainty,” she told Reuters yesterday, pointing to a variety of risk factors, including geopolitical tensions, technological advancements, climate shocks and demographic shifts. “All of this means that after we recover from this shock, we need to keep our eyes open for the next one.”

Current Fed policy is still modestly tight, based on a simple model using the unemployment rate and the year-over-year change in the headline Consumer Price Index. That gives the central bank space to take a wait-and-see approach and monitor incoming numbers.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, however, suggests that a rate hike could be near. Asked to characterize the state of risk for the economic outlook using a four-color template, ranging from “the house is on fire” red to “everything is looking swell” green, he responded. “At least orange. Orange with ​a chance of meatballs; it hasn’t been great.”

The Treasury market is inclined to agree lately. But inflation and economic data arrive with a lag, and so the Fed will continue to wait to develop a clearer view of how the economy’s reacting to the war with Iran.

The challenge is not waiting too long, lest inflation and/or slower growth move too far ahead of policy, which would leave the Fed in the difficult position of trying to play catch-up. That was the case when the central bank was slow to raise rates when inflation spiked in 2021-2022, and so avoiding that mistake is very much a priority just a few years after that policy mistake.

At the same time, the risk of acting too early could make a bad situation worse, on either the inflation or growth fronts.

In the end, the Fed’s challenge is less about choosing the perfect policy setting and more about staying nimble in a world where the ground keeps shifting. The only certainty is that the next move—whenever it comes—will be made under conditions that are anything but certain.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why You Should Still Stay Out Of Alexandra Real Estate
data.description:
On our previous coverage of Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (ARE), we identified the potential for a shorter term bounce.

Our experience is that you can get a short bounce here, maybe after 2–3 days of selling.

Source: It's Not The Dividend Cut, It's The Debt Load

But our longer term view of the company remained firmly negative.

The fact is that today they forecasted worse than almost all analysts and their leverage dials are moving up despite $3.0 billion of expected sales. You got to be a brave soul to think this is "an accumulating opportunity." Where do we stand? This is even more untouchable from a longer-term perspective than at any point in our previous coverage.

Source: It's Not The Dividend Cut, It's The Debt Load

Since that article came out, ARE did indeed validate our conclusions on both timeframes.

It bottomed shortly after and did a rip-roaring rally to suck in the next round of investors. It is now, even lower than before. We go over some key thoughts here and tell you why we believe a wipe-out remains probable.

Supply & Demand

If you go by ARE's 2026 guidance from their Q4-2025 conference call, we are looking for a dip in the occupancy rate in Q1-2026, followed by some growth in the back half of 2026. Our view is that we will certainly get that dip, but the growth in the back half won't materialize. What is more interesting here is that ARE plans to unload $2.6 billion worth of properties in 2026. While this does sound like an impressive figure, it is rendered less impressive by two factors. First the amount of construction spending that ARE has planned for 2026.

Second, the total amount of debt it actually has. In fact, the cumulative impact from its asset sales and declining net operating income (NOI), will mean another increase in debt to EBITDA.

But where the problems for ARE really shine through, are in the overall trajectory for supply and demand. We look at this through a few different metrics. The easiest amongst these is the rent spread on new leases. ARE publishes this every quarter and you can see the drop in the new rates when you compare Q4-2025 to the full year 2025. The comparison is even worse relative to full year 2024.

This is in our view the biggest smoking gun and one which investors tend to ignore. We see this number as the trade-off between maintaining occupancy and losing it. ARE might be able to keep occupancy from falling, but then that would mean horrendous cash and GAAP spreads.

Of course this leads to the question, why would occupancy fall further under status quo? Well, last time around, we mentioned the Nature poll showing that a vast majority of US scientists were done with this administration's policy of favoring fantasy over science and were considering leaving the country. Of course there is a difference between intentions and actions. But people are following through. Where feasible, they are choosing other destinations. Of course, the current administration is working extra hard to make the problem worse for ARE.

Actually those firings were just the start. The proposed 2027 budget, if executed, would mean annihilation for the bull prospects.

Where is all this money going?

The budget would increase funding for presidential priorities – such as the military, which would receive US $1.5 trillion, a 44% increase – while reining in spending on many domestic programs.

Source: Nature

While this looks bad, it is pretty standard modus operandi for the current administration. For example, the FDA had prevented 14 peptides from being produced by pharmacies in 2023.

The FDA in ​2023 removed 14 peptides from a list of ​products that it allows compounding pharmacies to produce. The compounded peptides were commonly used in wellness, integrative medicine ​and sports medicine.

The agency had said the move ​was based on significant safety risks including risks related to ‌immunogenicity, ⁠toxicity and impurity of the peptides.

Source: Reuters

The writing was on the wall for some time and they are now back.

Kennedy recently declared himself "a big fan" of peptides, telling podcast host Joe Rogan that he's personally used them to treat injuries. He also vowed to loosen FDA limits on producing injectable peptides, which have been subject to federal safety restrictions.

Some of Kennedy's allies and supporters are proponents of the chemicals, including Gary Brecka, a self-described "longevity expert," who sells peptide injectables, patches and nasal sprays through his website.

Source: PBS

The reversal (unbanning) marked another step to alienate the actual scientists. But Congress is fighting back, for once.

We still think the overall trajectory for science is lower, though it may stop at just abysmal and not make it all the way to the stone age.

Meanwhile, property values are plummeting.

BURLINGTON, MA–Lithium-ion battery manufacturer A123 Systems has acquired a converted lab property in Burlington, Massachusetts, at a steep discount, highlighting the cooling of the Greater Boston lab market.

As reported by Bisnow, A123 Systems purchased the 115,000-square-foot lab conversion at 10 Corporate Drive for nearly $20M, according to public records. The seller, The Gutierrez Co., bought the property in 2021 for $42M, meaning the asset traded at roughly a 52% discount.

The Gutierrez Co. converted the former office building into lab space in 2021, during a period of strong demand for laboratory space and widespread office-to-lab conversions. According to Bisnow, market conditions have since shifted, with rising lab vacancy and a glut of new supply making such conversion projects less attractive to traditional life sciences tenants.

Source: Boston Real Estate

ARE is itself jumping ship on projects where it can.

The way all of this plays out is likely through slow and steady declines in occupancy and rents for ARE. It does not even have to be a lot. Most REITs tend to go under near the 80% occupancy levels where they have to restructure their debts. The reason is that 6X debt to EBITDA is extremely unforgiving on the downside. NAV estimates are moving lower but remained unaligned with reality.

We see this go even lower over time. This will be similar to the move for Allied Properties (AP.UN:CA) where analysts were going in the right direction, but underestimating the magnitude of the move.

Verdict

In our view, we are fairly close to both rating agencies biting the bullet and starting ARE down the road to junk. ARE does have one of the best balance sheets as far as length of debt maturities. But a deteriorating outlook more than makes up for it in the long run. But that long length might allow this story to continue for a few more years. It is also possible that things change after the next election. But at present, there is no reason to get involved outside of trading rallies.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Energy Stocks: Winners And Losers At The Beginning Of Q2 2026
data.description:
For a long time energy stocks were something of a bore, which meant that not much time or attention was spent on them as they did not do all that much to warrant it. However, energy stocks were able to achieve something in Q1 2026 that has been unusual to see in recent years, which is to rise to the top of the leaderboard with gains that far surpass that of the leading indexes. Energy stocks have outperformed thus far in 2026 with a major assist from recent events in the Middle East and the reaction for many may be to jump into the fray. However, there is an argument to be made that recent events are not as positive for the sector as it may appear at first. In fact, they may even serve to backfire down the road.

Energy stocks come to life

A previous article at the start of 2026 written by myself noted how the energy sector had underperformed in what was a good year overall for most stocks in 2025. In addition, the year 2026 looked destined to turn into another year of weak performance, especially with the oil market projected to be oversupplied. Yet the article still argued that the year 2026 was not guaranteed to play out the same way as 2025. For this to happen and for energy stocks to surprise by outperforming, it was most likely to happen as a result of possible moves on the geopolitical chessboard, specifically in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz in particular.

It is still early and there are three more quarters to go in 2026, but the energy sector did much better in Q1 2026 than it ever did in 2025. For instance, the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE), an ETF which tracks companies active in the energy sector, has tended to lag well behind the SPDR S&P500 ETF (SPY) in recent quarters, including as recently as Q4 2025.

However, this changed in Q1 2026 because XLE ended the quarter with a gain of 37.02%, much better than the 4.63% loss for SPY. This includes a gain of 9.55% in the month of March, a very turbulent month for energy markets. The chart above shows how XLE has tended to not do much, only to come roaring back to life in Q1 2026.

XLE would not have accomplished this if the geopolitical situation was not as tense as it was during the quarter and if tensions had not morphed into an all-out war in the Middle East. This led to Iran imposing restrictions on the ability of ships to sail through the Strait of Hormuz, which, together with attacks on energy infrastructure in the region, caused oil prices to spike higher. The chart below shows how crude oil rose during the first two months of 2026, only to go almost vertical in March as the war with Iran got underway.

It is worth noting that while the energy sector, as represented by XLE, has appreciated quite significantly, it has not appreciated as much as crude oil. The disparity became even more stark in March when the fighting broke out. XLE itself appreciated by 9.55% during March, which is not bad, but it pales in comparison to the price increases for crude oil.

Some energy stocks managed to do even better

XLE was not a bad way to play the rise in the energy sector, but even higher returns were possible with exposure to individual stocks. This includes the ones present in XLE. XLE is an ETF tracking 22 stocks and they are Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), ConocoPhillips (COP), EOG Resources (EOG), Schlumberger (SLB), The Williams Companies (WMB), Valero Energy (VLO), Phillips 66 (PSX), Marathon Petroleum (MPC), Kinder Morgan (KMI), Baker Hughes (BKR), Oneok Inc (OKE), Targa Resources (TRGP), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), EQT Corp (EQT), Diamondback Energy (FANG), Halliburton (HAL), Devon Energy (DVN), Coterra Energy (CTRA), Texas Pacific Land Corp (TPL), Expand Energy Corp (EXE) and APA Corp (APA). The table above shows how half or 11 out of the 22 stocks listed above managed to beat XLE in terms of gains in Q1 2026, led by APA with a gain of 73.51%, TPL with a gain of 65.23% and OXY with a gain of 58.07%. Still, not all energy stocks fared well in what was an exceptional quarter because EXE lost 0.53% during the quarter.

This goes up to 3 out of 22 that lost ground if only the month of March, or when the war in the Middle East got underway, is considered, namely TPL, BKR and WMB. Another two, SLB and KMI came very close to ending March in the red. APA was the standout performer with a gain of 39.74% in March, well ahead of runner-ups MPC with a gain of 23.19% and OXY with a gain of 22.46%.

In other words, if APA is excluded as it was very much an outlier in March, XLE did not benefit as much from the outbreak of the fighting, in contrast to oil futures. If someone wanted to take optimal advantage of the present situation in energy markets and the war in the Middle East in terms of returns, then playing crude oil itself was the way to go. Energy stocks, not so much.

What to look for in Q2 2026

The outperformance on the part of the energy sector stands out because it could have turned out very differently if there had been no war in the Middle East. Both the IEA and OPEC called for the oil market to be oversupplied heading into 2026, but the restrictions imposed by Iran on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz have turned the supply and demand equation completely upside down.

It’s clear that if not for the geopolitical factor, energy stocks would have fared worse than they did in Q1 2026. It is the war in the Middle East that is pushing up the sector and with the war still ongoing, it is tempting to place bets on energy stocks in Q2 2026. However, how Q2 2026 turns out is not pre-determined as there are a number of factors that could sway how the quarter turns out.

There have been attacks on energy-related infrastructure throughout the Middle East that could have a lingering impact. Qatar, for example, has announced that some of the damage done to its LNG facilities could take years to repair. If this continues and more production facilities go offline, energy markets will remain in a crisis even if the Strait of Hormuz is fully re-opened.

Given the current situation, crude prices are on the rise and they may very well have yet to peak. One of the more immediate measures taken after the fighting broke out was for countries to release supplies from oil reserves. This includes the U.S. with its strategic petroleum reserve. However, unless more reserves are made available, the impact is set to recede in Q2 2026.

It has also been a month since the war started, which means most of the tankers currently stuck in the Persian Gulf were supposed to arrive at their destination sometime in Q2 2026, had there been no war. While some have been able to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Q2 2026 is when the market will start feeling the impact of tankers not making delivery. All this suggests oil and gas prices are likely to rise much higher in Q2 2026.

However, while it is tempting to bet on higher energy prices, especially with the prior points in mind, higher prices are still not a foregone conclusion, let alone any specific number, because it depends on how the war in the Middle East evolves. Iran, for instance, has effectively seized control of the Strait of Hormuz, which allows it to decide how much oil enters world markets.

This means Iran can increase global prices, but Iran can also decrease prices if it increases the number of ships that have been allowed to cross. While Iran benefits from higher oil prices, Iran also needs to have certain countries on its side, China in particular. Iran has a better chance of achieving this if it allows supplies to these countries to pass through. This will constrain the rise of global energy prices, at least to a certain extent.

Still, no factor is likely to exert greater sway in terms of how Q2 2026 turns out for the energy sector than how the war itself evolves. At this time, the most probable scenario is for higher energy prices, but this can change if the war is perceived to be close to an end. The U.S. is trying to degrade Iran’s ability to launch attacks to the point that it can no project power into the region, which includes damaging facilities in the region and blocking the Strait of Hormuz.

But whether the U.S. succeeds in doing this in Q2 2026 is up in the air. According to most pre-war estimates, Iran was believed to have an arsenal of several thousands of ballistic missiles. If this is accurate, and it can be assumed that a portion is no longer available, either because they have been used by Iran or destroyed by the U.S./Israel, then it is possible Iran may run out or at least run short sometime in Q2 2026. This would affect Iran’s ability to continue the war. Iran also has drones, but these are not as difficult to stop. A defeated Iran would cause energy prices to crater and energy stocks would not be far behind.

Yet it needs mentioning that the numbers are just estimates and it is not impossible that Iran has a much greater inventory than believed. Iran can manufacture its own weapons, although it probably needs to import certain components. On the other hand, the same can be said of the U.S. since it too is expending its inventory of weapons.

If the war devolves into a war of attrition and both sides seek to outlast the other, Iran will need to find a way to get re-supplied and replenish what has been expended. This is where Russian and China can play a key role because with their support, Iran stands a much better chance of getting the supplies it needs to continue the war. Russia in particular has a lot of reasons for wanting Iran to continue to fight.

The goal for the U.S. is to degrade Iran’s military to the point that it is effectively defeated, but for Iran the goal is to deny the U.S. from achieving this objective, whether or not in collaboration with other countries. If Iran is to come out on top, it will probably require China’s and Russia’s support. They don’t have to get directly involved, but they do need to keep supplies flowing to Iran to allow it to continue the fight.

What this all suggests that while the most probable outcome is for energy stocks to move higher in Q2 2026, it is not a guaranteed bet as the possibility exists for energy prices to go down and take the energy sector down with it. While the first looks more likely given the present situation, either outcome is possible in Q2 2026.

Why there could be a further divergence between energy prices and energy stocks

There is another scenario worth taking into account when deciding whether to be betting on the energy sector in Q2 2026. It is not impossible for the energy sector to lag behind energy prices, even if the war intensifies and energy prices soar higher. Keep in mind that ETF’s like XLE consists primarily of U.S. companies.

The issue here is that Iran could specifically target U.S. interests, which would hurt American companies and that could affect how XLE trades since attacks will lead to financial losses for companies. Iran is already doing this to some extent, including by letting say oil tankers from certain countries unscathed, while not doing the same to those tied to the U.S.

In the worst case, Iran could be perceived to be winning the what is currently undecided conflict. If this happens, the long-term consequences would be very negative for not just the U.S., but also U.S. companies doing business in the region and that includes those in XLE. There is more at risk for XLE than the headline gains for XLE in Q1 2026 may indicate.

Why the oil crisis may come back to haunt the energy sector in the long term

How Q2 2026 ultimately plays out has yet to be determined, both in terms of the war itself, energy prices and the energy sector. It is not guaranteed, but energy bulls have the better odds as to who gets rewarded in Q1 2026, as things currently stand. However, with that said, there is reason to think that even if the energy sector, as covered by XLE, continues to be a winner in the short term, it might also lose out in the long term.

The current environment of very high energy prices is very unlikely to illicit no response. One consequence is that there will be renewed attempts to reduce reliance on the Middle East for energy supplies, both oil and gas. Some of the Gulf States are likely to try to reduce their reliance on the Strait of Hormuz by creating alternative transport routes using pipelines, but this cannot be a final solution because pipelines are still subject to attacks from drones or what not that can disrupt supplies.

If Iran’s leverage with the Strait of Hormuz is to be reduced, it will require alternative sources of energy that do not reside in the region. This means more investments, especially from the oil majors, in exploration and/or exploitation. Capex is likely to increase and that could impact the balance sheet. North America is likely to play a key role when it comes to increasing supplies outside of the Middle East.

The goal is to increase supplies from outside the Middle East so as the reduce potential fallout from anything happening in that specific region. There is a historical precedent for this happening. For instance, OPEC supplied slightly more than half the world’s oil in 1973 when the oil crisis erupted later that year. The resulting oil embargo drove Western countries in particular to look for alternative sources, which eventually resulted in OPEC gradually losing market share in the following decades.

OPEC’s response was to bring in other countries, Russia in particular, by creating OPEC+, or an expanded set of oil-producing countries. This restored the new entity’s ability to influence prices by controlling roughly half of world production at the time it was founded. If history repeats and the reaction to the current oil shock is similar to the oil shock of the seventies, energy supplies from non-traditional actors are likely to grow quickly in the coming years. The Guyana basin is a candidate here.

The flip side of this is that it could lead to depressed prices as the market could get oversupplied. OPEC+ and non-member suppliers could begin to duke it out. This would hurt the energy sector by essentially recreating the conditions that had become rather prevalent before the current oil shock provided relief. So while energy stocks may be celebrating what is currently going on in the world, they may not be a few down years down the road when consequences from the latest oil stock start to make their impact felt.

Another way XLE could get adversely affected worth mentioning is by increased production from renewable energy sources like solar energy. On the demand side, there will likely be attempts to drive down the need for oil and gas with the use of electrical vehicles and so on. The current energy crisis, which has led to higher prices and/or shortages, will increase the incentive to switch towards alternative energy sources that are less likely to be disrupted by a war in the Middle East. This will affect an ETF like XLE that is weighted towards companies active in the oil and gas sector with XOM and CVX accounting for 41% by weight.

Investor takeaways

The energy sector was a clear winner in Q1 2026, thanks to an assist from recent developments in the Middle East, and it is tempting to bet on a repeat of Q1 2026 in Q2 2026. The war continues and while the U.S. would probably like there to be a halt to the fighting, provided it leads to the restoration of the situation as it was before the war, which includes the free movement of ships through the Strait of Hormuz, the same may not necessarily be true of Iran.

Iran is more likely to see a continuation of the war in its interest because the full impact of the disruptions to the oil and gas market in the past 6 weeks or so have yet to be felt. The Gulf States are said to be running low on interceptors to defend themselves against Iranian attacks, which means that attacks on energy infrastructure and so on could become more effective in Q2 2026 compared to Q1 2026.

The U.S. is busy degrading Iran’s military, but there is no sign that Iran has lost the ability to fight. This favors a continuation of the war in Q2 2026, which in turn favors upwards movement in energy prices and the energy sector by extension. It is thus tempting to be long energy stocks, like those represented in an ETF like XLE, but there is a risk to doing so because of the possibility the war could go in a direction that is not readily apparent at the start of Q2 2026. This includes an end, whether temporary or not, to the fighting.

This would not be well received by the energy sector, especially since without this war, the energy sector would likely be underperforming, like it did before. While this is arguably less likely to happen than the more likely scenario of higher energy prices due to continued fighting, it is a possibility that cannot be ruled out and therefore must be accounted for.

How the war itself evolves will determine the path of energy prices, and by extension what kind of quarter it will be for the energy sector in Q2 2026. I would therefore hold on to existing positions, but I would not be a buyer of more shares, not after the way they have rallied by as much as they already have. It is not impossible for such a move to pay off, but it depends too much on something happening, which is for the war to continue, something not at all guaranteed. If for whatever reason this does not happen, the energy sector can expect to perform like it did in recent quarters, which is not that good.

data.title: TYG: Can See Durable Growth Over The Coming Decade
data.description:
The Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp (TYG) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with exposure to master limited partnerships [MLPs] in the energy infrastructure market, earning investors capital growth and distribution income. The strategy employs leverage, allowing the fund to provide enhanced exposure to the market while facilitating a higher earning potential. With increasing demand for natural gas resources in the US and overseas, I believe the next wave of growth investments will set the stage for renewed momentum in the industry, presenting investors with an appealing investment approach to capital-heavy, oil & gas midstream companies. Given the potential market tailwinds to come, I am recommending TYG with a Buy rating.

Investment Case for TYG

TYG currently provides investors with exposure to midstream gas as well as power infrastructure, a market with an appealing backdrop underpinned by growing electricity demand in the US. Driving demand growth is the combination of reindustrialization and the development of large-scale data centers to power AI. Thematically, these two factors coincide as both a user of AI and a developer/facilitator of AI. From the industrial standpoint, the market firmly believes that new and existing facilities will lean into automation and industrial robotics to deliver factory output optimization, which may inadvertently lead to increased power needs to operate these autonomous machines.

The data center growth story presents substantial demand for electricity with a single compound requiring the amount of power to light up a small city. Many of the facilities being constructed are 200MW+ with larger facilities being constructed with 1GW+ of capacity, potentially requiring a dedicated, behind-the-meter power supply before transitioning to grid load. Regardless of the path of power, more infrastructure will be necessary to light up these facilities, driving a renewed growth path for companies operating the infrastructure and producing the power.

Another growth theme that is emerging as a more critical component of the domestic gas theme is how the Iranian war may shape the future of LNG supply. Prior to the war, Qatar supplied 20% of the world’s LNG cargoes before missile attacks and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Deepening the global gas uncertainty, gas fields and infrastructure have been a primary target between both war powers with Saudi Arabia and the UAE also halting production following a missile strike on Iranian gas fields.

While we shouldn’t expect an immediate impact to the domestic gas market, I believe that if the Iranian war progresses beyond weeks/months, the supply chain for LNG cargoes will need to be rewritten to derisk regional exposure, potentially creating an appealing opportunity for domestic exporters to accelerate the development of new liquefaction facilities. This, in turn, can deliver growth across the value chain, particularly for interstate pipeline and natural gas gathering and processing operators. Though planning and development may take years before capital commitments, the shift towards growth can be appealing for long-term investors, particularly when considering the long-term distributable cash flow growth that may come.

Overall, companies held in TYG should benefit from the growing demand for electricity, driving the need for growth capital investments that can be viewed as long-term expansionary for cash generation.

About Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp

TYG was launched on February 24, 2004 by Tortoise Capital as a closed-end fund. The strategy has a management fee of 95bps, not inclusive of leverage fees and other operating fees.

The strategy is more heavily allocated to common shares at 78% with MLPs accounting for 20% of the portfolio weight; when applying leverage, TYG provided 101.5% exposure to common shares and 26% exposure to MLPs as of December 31, 2025. TYG employs a leverage rate of 24.6% to enhance asset exposure, allowing the fund to provide greater investment opportunities to its shareholders.

Breaking down the portfolio, TYG is most heavily exposed to renewables and power infrastructure, accounting for 54.2% of common equity exposure. Top holdings within the industry include Clearway Energy (CWEN), Evergy (EVRG), and Sempra Energy (SRE), providing exposure to diversified power production in renewable resources, natural gas, and coal.

As for midstream infrastructure, TYG provides significant exposure to Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX (MPLX), and The Williams Companies (WMB), providing investors with diversified exposure to natural gas pipelines and gathering & processing as well as refined products pipelines.

In addition to owning shares and units, TYG may write call options on shares held in order to enhance distributions throughout the year. In FY25, TYG wrote call options on Core Scientific (CORZ), First Solar (FSLR), and Femi.

TYG transitioned to a monthly distribution at the start of 2025 after merging with the Tortoise Midstream Energy Fund. Distributions have exhibited annual growth since 2021 following the near collapse of the US O&G market. The distribution presently pays out $5.70/share, yielding 12.25% on a forward basis, presenting an appealing investment opportunity for income-seeking investors. A major advantage to holding TYG is that income is reported on a 1099 rather than a K-1.

Risks Related to TYG

TYG is a non-diversified portfolio strategy that is heavily exposed to the energy commodity market, presenting certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. While many midstream operators have transitioned towards a fee-based revenue strategy, earning tariff fees on transported volumes, the market may be exposed to the impacts of negative commodity pricing performance, particularly if volume production were to be scaled back. Investors will be exposed to manager risk, relying on the abilities of the portfolio management team to appropriately select and weight equities and units. Distributions may exhibit a high return of capital given the nature of distributable income across the portfolio holdings.

Final Thoughts

TYG can be an appealing investment strategy for investors seeking domestic energy exposure, particularly when considering the growth outlay for supplying capacity for the increased shift in power demand tied to reindustrialization and large-scale data center development. While TYG may not offer the same level of high-growth as other sectors, the high level of distributed income and durability brings a certain appeal that can provide investors with more stability in their portfolio strategies. Given that this is a non-diversified portfolio, TYG should be held as a satellite position as part of a diversified portfolio; I am recommending TYG with a Buy rating.

data.title: AMC Entertainment Put On A Great Show
data.description:
April 6th was a really great day for shareholders of AMC Entertainment Holdings, Inc. (AMC). Even though the stock is still down 21.3% since I last wrote about it in early January of this year, it skyrocketed 12.5% after management revealed just how strong the Easter weekend was for the business. This is great in and of itself. And it furthers my own view that the company makes more of a Hold candidate than it does a Buy or Sell.

To be clear, the firm still continues to face certain challenges. Management is cutting out underperforming locations, and revenue and profits are coming in mixed. Sales in particular have taken a hit because of the reduction in the number of movie theaters that it has in operation. That leads to a reduction in attendance. Having said that, box office data is looking promising. And I would make the case that the business has already made it through the worst of the issues that it is likely to contend with. Given all of this, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the appropriate course of action.

An interesting movie

Shares of AMC Entertainment closed up 12.5% on April 6th after management revealed just how strong the Easter weekend was for them. According to management, over 6 million moviegoers attended one of the company's theaters globally. This was for the window of time from April 1st through April 5th. Management described it as the highest global attendance for a Wednesday through Sunday in 2026. But that's not all. The firm actually went a step further and said that it was the highest combined global admissions and food and beverage revenue that the firm had ever seen for the Easter holiday in the 106 year history of the business.

A big driver of this happened to be The Super Mario Galaxy movie, which brought in $190.8 million at the domestic box office and $181.8 million in international markets. This works out to $372.6 million. And honestly, it's a little surprising. The movie actually received really awful reviews. According to IMDb it scored 6.5 out of 10. And on Rotten Tomatoes, it scored a 42% approval rating. The comments that I looked at described the film as visually stunning, but derivative and more of an advertisement for the brand more so than a compelling story.

I have not seen it, as I don't really watch movies much these days, so I am not positioned to opine on it. But what I do know is that the first film really didn't do great, either. When it came out in 2023, The Super Mario Bros. Movie ended up going on to get just 7 out of 10 on IMDb and it scored an approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes of 59%. That’s certainly better than the second movie, but far from great. And yet, it went on to generate $1.36 billion in the global box office, with $574.9 million of that in the domestic space. It appears as though one thing that certainly helped the company out was the fact that the merchandise program that the company had involving the film ended up being the second-best selling and second-highest grossing merchandise program of all time for the business, coming in only behind Taylor Swift / The Eras Tour. There were other contributors to the strong weekend as well, but it's clear what the standout feature was.

The fact of the matter is that for years, the movie theater industry was under significant pressure. Initially this was caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. But then, just as conditions were improving, strikes in Hollywood caused significant disruptions for the space. This year, however, it looks as though we could finally see a true recovery in the space.

In the table above, you can see domestic box office revenue for the first thirteen weeks of this year compared to the same time of last year. I also included data for 2024. So far, box office revenue is up 29.2% year over year, with some weeks showing a near doubling in revenue. This is fantastic to see, and it marks a significant improvement from the decline that we saw the same time between 2024 and 2025.

The big question is what the future will hold for AMC Entertainment specifically. The company continues to face challenges. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the most recent quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. You can also see results for 2025 as a whole compared to 2024 in the chart below. Revenue did increase from 2024 to 2025. But for the final quarter of each year, we actually saw a contraction in sales. According to management, this decline in revenue was the result of fewer and less compelling movie releases. Specifically, the North American box office dropped 4.4% year over year. Clearly, the most recent data shows this to have been just a temporary weakness. But it doesn't change the fact that it caused some pain, leading to a reduction in cash flows and adjusted net income.

The week box office showing pushed attendance down from 62,424 to 56,325. This was clear in not only the U.S., but international markets as well. It also didn't help that management continues to reduce the number of locations in operation as the firm looks to slowly transform itself into a healthy business once again. Management even described their efforts as a relentless focus when it comes to achieving operating performance improvements and to optimize the portfolio of assets that the company has. On top of this, management continues to invest capital into improving the guest experience, including through loyalty programs, an innovative food and beverage variety, and continued growth in the premium large format space.

The good news is that, even though attendance recently took a hit, average ticket prices have continued to rise. Given how weak the economy is in the U.S. right now, it is very possible that some of the drop in attendance is because of stresses imposed on consumers. But that is more of a speculative guess on my end than it is concrete evidence backed by management. Moving forward, management does need to continue cutting costs, even if that might mean that growth prospects at a time when the box office is exploding might be more limited.

Given the profitability that the company achieved during the final quarter of 2025, we can't really value the company in any way other than on an EV to EBITDA basis. Right now, the firm is trading at a multiple of 11.2. I wouldn't necessarily call that expensive, but for the amount of net debt that the business has, $3.61 billion weighed against its market capitalization of $734.3 million, I would say it's certainly not cheap enough to turn bullish on. But the good news is that it certainly isn't in the overvalued range either.

In fact, it is possible, if management can benefit from this surge in the box office that we have seen so far this year, that the stock could eventually become cheap enough to warrant an upgrade. In the chart below, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA the business we need to generate in order to be at least fairly valued at a price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple of between 5 and 15 and at an EV to EBITDA multiple in the same range. Getting into the single digits certainly not a big stretch here, especially for a firm that in 2025 generated $4.85 billion of revenue.

What is certainly exciting here is that management believes, at least as of the time that they announced results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, that the North American box office will be stronger this year than last year, which is exactly what we are seeing take place right now. They said, for instance, that the box office here at home would likely be between $500 million and $1 billion above what it was last year. Timing differences can absolutely affect box office results from one week to the next. But are ready for the first 13 weeks of this year, the box office is $393.9 million above what it was the same time last year. So we do certainly seem to be heading in the right direction.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I believe that the market is finally starting to acknowledge that the worst is likely over for AMC Entertainment. To be clear, the business still has plenty of issues. Debt is the biggest one, while negative cash flows would be the second worst. However, the firm continues to make adjustments in improving operations while the industry itself is showing really strong signs of returning back to the kinds of activity that we saw before the pandemic. In light of all of these positives, I have no choice but to keep the company rated a Hold.

data.title: The Biggest Risk For Covered Call ETF Investors And How To Avoid It
data.description:
Introduction

Covered call ETF products serve as a meaningful source of income for many dividend-seeking investors.

The monthly current income streams that usually yield over 10% make these products particularly attractive for investors who want to receive the biggest bang for the buck (i.e., high incremental income impact from each dollar deployed) without involving significant NAV-erosion risks.

Now, the latter part of the statement above is what ultimately gets sacrificed by the key (and I'd say the most frequent) mistake that many covered call ETF investors make.

Based on hundreds of discussions (via my covered call ETF comment sections) with my followers and, importantly, the market data, I can say that more often than not investors have unintentionally assumed too heavy concentration risk into particular market segment - i.e., the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) and/or the S&P 500 (SPY), which are biased towards large-cap growth factor.

Currently, I can see that a heavy price for that is being paid.

In the article below I will try to highlight the problem in an easy-to-understand way and then close the piece with my strategic recommendation on how the situation could be improved.

Problem or the #1 mistake

By investing in covered call ETF products, many investors have not only integrated higher-yielding monthly income sources into their portfolios but also introduced a heavier emphasis on the large-cap growth factor.

As stated above, it is something that I have concluded from my discussions with the followers (those who invest and have shared their covered call ETF picks). But, obviously, just basing my assumption on this observation would be too subjective and/or speculative.

However, the math, or the numbers, doesn't lie. And here they come.

In the table below I've gathered Top 10 largest covered call ETFs out there:

Ticker AuM Exposure
JEPI $43.9B S&P 500
JEPQ $34.5B Nasdaq-100
QQQI $9.4B Nasdaq-100
SPYI $8.2B S&P 500
QYLD $8.1B Nasdaq-100
DIVO $6.7B 24 large-cap names
KNG $3.4B S&P 500 (aristocrats)
GPIX $3.2B S&P 500
GPIQ $3.1B Nasdaq-100
XYLD $3.0B S&P 500

The conclusions are clear.

Almost all of the Top 10 (in terms of AUM) covered call ETF instruments are linked to either SPY or QQQ. The First Trust Vest S&P 500® Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (KNG) and the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) have a bit of a different angle, but at their core they are still tracking large-cap exposures that stem from the same S&P 500 names.

To illustrate why this might be a problem, I've selected Top 5 names (not all 10 to not overpopulate the chart) and measured their 1-month performance:

The correlations are very strong.

Granted, there are some differences that can come from the applied strategy and what type of options contracts are sold. The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) is a good example, as it sells at-the-money covered calls, which set a more aggressive upside ceiling but in return provide greater option premium income that tends to work well in falling share price environments.

However, while there indeed are differences, the daylight between ATM (or zero-day expiration) and out-of-the-money covered call ETFs is not that wide.

If the underlying, which in this case is either SPY or QQQ, plunges, the covered call ETFs (irrespective of their option strategy) will follow accordingly. Namely, the covered call ETF products that share the same underlying have rather similar downside risk.

Now, what does it mean? Well, I can see at least the following three consequences:

1. Investors who already have opened pure-play exposures to these (or any other large-cap growth-focused) have magnified the portfolio concentration risk (downside risk) to a particular factor.

2. Investors who have spread their covered call ETF exposures across many different names in order to diversify have potentially failed to do so.

3. Investors have leaned into, arguably, one of the most volatile and risky factors: the large-cap growth factor. In environments like this, when the recessionary risks are looming and the uncertainty levels are elevated, growth tends to be one of the first in line to experience negative repricing. The fact that we entered 2026 with a) 3 strong bull years behind us and b) very high valuations does not help either.

Some might argue here that recently there has been significant covered call ETF product innovation, with many fresh names being sent to the market.

While I agree that this is the case and that in some instances the underlying exposures do indeed differ, the vast majority of the new products are still somehow tied to the same SPY and/or QQQ indices.

For example, not that long ago, YieldMax launched a series of new single-security covered call ETF instruments. Yet, the lion's share of them sit in the Nasdaq-100 index: e.g., YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY), YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY), the YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (PLTY), etc.

Even the largest 0DTE covered call ETFs - the Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strat ETF (QDTE) and the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) - are connected to QQQ and SPY, respectively.

And again, if SPY or QQQ falls, then also these products will follow accordingly (the stock-specific covered call ETFs look so bad because their underlyings have experienced significant pullbacks - worse than for the overall indices):

Finally, what I could label as the absolute worst diversification element (if the idea was to diversify the covered call ETF portfolio bucket) is the addition of leveraged covered call ETFs (which sell option contracts against an underlying that is magnified by external leverage).

The NEOS Boosted Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (XQQI) and the NEOS Boosted S&P 500 High Income ETF (XSPI) - both leveraged covered call ETFs against QQQ and SPY, respectively - tell the story very nicely:

Recommended action plan

My suggestion for investors would be to take a fresh look at the covered call ETF investments and see how many of them boil down to the same underlying exposure. My educated guess is that the lion's share of them will be about SPY and/or QQQ indices.

While I fully acknowledge the differences among option contract strategies (e.g., OTM or ATM), I don't think that a diversification on a strategy level is what will move the needle in the context of downside protection (especially if we are talking about large and persistent drawdowns).

In this particular market environment, I wouldn't want to be overexposed to the large-cap growth-focused indices (in my case, it is max 10% to SPY/QQQ-tied names).

Now, I am not saying that all SPY/QQQ-linked covered call ETFs should be divested. In fact, I think that most income-oriented investors have an inherent underemphasis on large-cap growth names because in this area yields tend to be too small to make a difference. So investing in covered call ETFs such as the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) can offer a solution to that without sacrificing the necessary yield component.

However, what I am saying or suggesting is that investors revisit their covered call ETF allocations, contextualize them with the overall portfolio, and if the SPY/QQQ exposure jumps out as too heavy, then consider replacing them with other covered call ETFs that are completely detached from the large-cap growth factor.

There are some, what I call, value-oriented covered call ETFs that might come in handy for both diversification and enhanced downside protection (shield from growth sell-off) purposes. I've elaborated on the value-oriented covered call ETF strategy in my recent piece, Buy Value-Oriented Covered Call ETFs For Relaxed Passive Income.

Finally, let me wrap this article up by providing 5 covered call ETF names to consider further as alternatives to QQQ/SPY-linked peers:
